The corticobulbar innervation of facial motor neurons to muscles of the {{c1::lower::lower/upper}} face is {{c2::contra::ipsi/contra}}lateral

The {{c1::external}} ear consists of the auricle (pinna) and the external auditory meatus (auditory canal)
The function of the external ear is to transfer sound waves via {{c1::vibration}} of the eardrum
The external ear is separated from the middle ear by the {{c1::tympanic membrane}}
The {{c1::middle}} ear consists of the auditory ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes)
The middle ear is a(n) {{c1::air::air or fluid}}-filled space
The stapes inserts into the {{c1::oval}} window, a membrane between the middle and inner ear
The auditory ossicles of the middle ear conduct and {{c1::amplify}} sound from the eardrum to the inner ear
The {{c1::inner}} ear consists of a bony and membranous labyrinth
The {{c1::bony}} labyrinth of the inner ear consists of the semicircular canals, vestibule, and cochlea
The {{c1::membranous}} labyrinth of the inner ear consists of a series of ducts (scala vestibuli, scala tympani, scala media)
The inner ear is a(n) {{c1::fluid::air or fluid}}-filled space
The fluid in the scala {{c1::vestibuli}} and scala {{c1::tympani}} is called {{c2::perilymph}} (inner ear)
{{c2::Peri}}lymph (inner ear) is characterized by {{c1::high}} Na+ and {{c1::low}} K+ concentration
The fluid in the scala {{c1::media}} is called {{c2::endolymph}} (inner ear)
{{c2::Endo}}lymph (inner ear) is characterized by {{c1::low}} Na+ and {{c1::high}} K+ concentration
The {{c1::cochlea}} is a spiral-shaped structure in the inner ear that contains the organ of {{c2::Corti}}
The organ of Corti lies on the {{c1::basilar}} membrane of the cochlea and is bathed in the endolymph contained in the scala media
The organ of Corti contains two types of receptor cells: {{c1::inner}} and {{c1::outer hair}} cells
The inner and outer hair cells of the organ of Corti have {{c1::stereocilia}}, which make contact with the tectorial membrane
Bending of cilia in response to basilar membrane vibration produces a change in {{c1::K+}} conductance of the hair cell
{{c2::High}} frequencies are best heard at the {{c1::base}} of the cochlea (basilar membrane)
The {{c1::base}} of the cochlea (basilar membrane) is thin and rigid
{{c2::Low}} frequencies are best heard at the {{c1::apex}} of the basilar membrane (near the helicotrema)
The processing of different frequencies based on spatial arrangement is referred to as {{c1::tonotopy}}
Causes of {{c1::conductive}} hearing loss include obstruction, otosclerosis, and otitis media
Causes of {{c1::sensorineural}} hearing loss include damage to the cochlea, CN VIII, or central auditory connections
Noise-induced hearing loss is due to damage to stereociliated cells in the {{c1::organ of Corti}}

Which frequencies are typically lost first with noise-induced hearing loss?

{{c1::High frequencies::High or Low}}
The {{c1::spiral}} ganglion contains cell bodies whose peripheral axons innervate hair cells of the organ of Corti
The central axons from the spiral ganglion form the {{c1::cochlear}} part of CN VIII
All the axons in the cochlear part of CN VIII enter the {{c1::pontomedullary}} junction and synapse in the ventral and dorsal cochlear nuclei
Axons of cells in the cochlear nuclei {{c2::bi}}laterally innervate the {{c1::superior olivary}} nuclei in the pons
The auditory input from the superior olivary nucleus travels via the {{c1::lateral lemniscus}} to the {{c2::inferior colliculus}} (midbrain)
The inferior colliculus sends auditory information to the {{c1::medial geniculate body}} of the thalamus
The auditory information from the medial geniculate body projects to the {{c1::primary auditory cortex}} of the superior temporal gyrus
Lesions of the cochlear part of CN VIII or cochlear nuclei result in profound {{c1::uni::uni/bi}}lateral sensorineural hearing loss
Lesions at or above the superior olivary nucleus result in diminished hearing {{c1::bi::uni/bi}}laterally and decreased ability to localize sound

The semicircular canals of the vestibular system detect {{c1::angular}} acceleration of the head

*bonus-name of wall
The semicircular canals of the inner ear are filled with {{c1::endo}}lymph
At the end of each semicircular canal (ampulla) are {{c1::hair}} cells, which act as receptors
The cilia on the hair cells of the semicircular canal are embedded in a gelatinous structure called the {{c1::cupula}}
The hair cells of the semicircular canals have a single long cilium, called a(n) {{c1::kinocilium}}, and several smaller {{c2::stereocilia}}
If the stereocilia are bent toward the kinocilium, the hair cell {{c1::de}}polarizes
If the stereocilia are bent away from the kinocilium, the hair cell {{c1::hyper}}polarizes
The {{c2::utricle}} and {{c2::saccule}} (otolith organs) of the vestibular system detect {{c1::linear}} acceleration of the head
Secondary vestibular fibers, arising from the vestibular nuclei, join the {{c1::medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF)}} and supply the motor nuclei of CN III, IV, and V
A lesion of the vestibular nuclei or nerve produces a vestibular-evoked {{c1::nystagmus}} (vestibulo-ocular reflex)
Vestibular-evoked nystagmus begins with a slow deviation {{c1::toward::toward or away from}} the lesion
Vestibular-evoked nystagmus is characterized by a fast corrective phase {{c1::away from::toward or away from}} the lesion
The {{c1::medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF)}} is a pair of tracts that allows for crosstalk between CN {{c2::VI}} and CN {{c2::III}} nuclei
One control center for horizontal gaze is located in the {{c1::frontal eye field}}, which is responsible for {{c2::contra}}lateral gaze
One control center for horizontal gaze is located in the {{c1::paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF)}}, which is responsible for {{c2::ipsi}}lateral gaze
The {{c1::abducens}} nucleus is coexistent with the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF), the center for ipsilateral horizontal gaze
The paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) sends axons to synapse with cell bodies of the ipsilateral {{c1::abducens}} nucleus
The abducens nucleus sends axons that course within the contralateral {{c2::medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF)}} to reach the {{c1::oculomotor}} nucleus
Lesions to the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) result in eyes that look {{c1::away from}} the side of the lesion
The medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) is {{c1::heavily::heavily or lightly}} myelinated

Are the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) lesions seen with multiple sclerosis classically uni- or bilateral?

{{c1::Bilateral}}
The directional term of internuclear ophthalmoplegia (e.g., right or left) refers to which eye is {{c1::paralyzed}}
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results in inability to {{c1::adduct}} the {{c2::ipsi}}lateral eye
Internuclear ophthalmoplegia results in {{c1::nystagmus}} of the {{c2::contra}}lateral eye
